It runs as a LangGraph app: each thread spawns its own isolated cloud sandbox, and the agent is invoked from Slack, Linear, or GitHub (PR comments, plus auto-review on opened / ready-for-review).\n\nA separate **reviewer** graph runs read-only code reviews on PRs, and a **review-style analyzer** graph learns per-repo review style from historical PRs.\n\n## Commands\n\nDependencies are managed with **uv**. Tests use pytest (`asyncio_mode = \"auto\"`). Lint/format is **ruff** (line-length 100, target py311). `requires-python = \">=3.11\"`; `langgraph.json` pins the runtime to 3.12.\n\n```bash\nmake install            # uv pip install -e .\nmake dev                # uv run langgraph dev — serves all three graphs + the FastAPI app from langgraph.json\nmake run                # uvicorn agent.webapp:app --reload --port 8000 (FastAPI only, no LangGraph runtime)\nmake test               # uv run pytest -vvv tests/\nmake test TEST_FILE=tests/test_open_pr_middleware.py    # single test file\nuv run pytest -vvv tests/test_open_pr_middleware.py::test_name  # single test\nmake lint               # ruff check + ruff format --diff\nmake format             # ruff format + ruff check --fix\n```\n\n`langgraph.json` declares three graph entrypoints and the FastAPI app, all served together by `langgraph dev`:\n\n| Graph | Entrypoint | Purpose |\n|---|---|---|\n| `agent` | `agent.server:get_agent` | Main coding agent (Slack/Linear/GitHub-triggered). |\n| `reviewer` | `agent.reviewer:get_reviewer_agent` | Read-only PR reviewer. Findings model + `publish_review`. |\n| `analyzer` | `agent.analyzer:get_analyzer` | Learns per-repo reviewer style from historical PRs and this reviewer's own finding outcomes. |\n| `ci_monitor` | `agent.ci_monitor:get_ci_monitor` | Polling fallback for CI auto-fix: each tick sweeps open agent-authored PRs for failing checks / merge conflicts via `agent.ci_autofix.sweep_open_prs`. |\n\nThe FastAPI app is `agent.webapp:app`.\n\nCI auto-fix (\"PR babysitting\") lives in `agent/ci_autofix.py`: when a CI check fails (webhook `check_run` / `check_suite` / `workflow_run` / `status`) or a reviewer leaves actionable feedback on a PR Open SWE opened, it locates the originating agent thread (by `pr_url` metadata) and dispatches a confidence-gated fix run on the `agent` graph. Gated by the per-user `auto_fix_ci` profile flag, the enabled-repos opt-in, and a per-PR `@open-swe autofix on|off` toggle (`agent/dashboard/autofix_state.py`). Skip-rules (base-branch failures, human commits, same-head dedupe, batching while runs are active, loop cap) all live in `ci_autofix.py`.\n\n## Architecture\n\n### Entrypoints\n\n- **`agent/server.py` → `get_agent(config)`** — main graph factory. Called per-thread. Resolves the GitHub token, gets-or-creates the sandbox for the thread, resolves the team/profile/per-thread model + effort, then constructs a fresh `create_deep_agent(...)` with the curated tool list and middleware stack. The agent itself is stateless — all per-thread state lives in the sandbox + thread metadata.\n- **`agent/reviewer.py` → `get_reviewer_agent(config)`** — reviewer graph factory. Shares `ensure_sandbox_for_thread` with the main agent but wires a reviewer-only toolset (`add_finding`, `update_finding`, `list_findings`, `publish_review`, `web_search`, `fetch_url`, `http_request`) and a different system prompt that pins the single-evolving-findings model and the diff-anchored bar for filing a finding. Read-only: no commit/push/PR-opening tools.\n- **`agent/analyzer.py` → `get_analyzer(config)`** — small graph that emits a per-repo style prompt via the `save_review_style_prompt` tool, consumed by the reviewer as a \"repository-specific review style\" appendix. It runs in one of two modes (`analyzer_mode` in `configurable`): **bootstrap** (cold-start: crawl historical PR reviews) and **continual** (nightly: refine using this reviewer's own finding outcomes via `read_finding_outcomes`). Each mode's procedure lives in a deepagents **skill** (`agent/skills/bootstrap-repo-analysis/`, `agent/skills/continual-learning/`) served as virtual files via a `CompositeBackend` `/skills/` route + `StateBackend` (seeded into the run's `files` channel by the launcher — never written to the sandbox). Launchers and the per-repo nightly cron live in `agent/dashboard/review_style_jobs.py` and `agent/dashboard/analyzer_cron.py`; the cron is registered when bootstrap completes.\n- **`agent/webapp.py`** — custom FastAPI routes mounted alongside the LangGraph server. Webhooks land here (GitHub, Linear, Slack). Each webhook resolves a deterministic `thread_id` (so follow-up messages route to the same agent run) and triggers/streams a run via the `langgraph_sdk` client. Also auto-reviews PRs on `opened` / `ready_for_review` events when the repo+author opt in.\n- **`agent/dashboard/`** — `router` mounted under the FastAPI app at startup (`app.include_router(dashboard_router)`). Owns GitHub OAuth, per-user profiles, admin endpoints, team defaults, enabled-repo lists, review-style management, and the Agents chat thread API used by the UI in `ui/`.\n\n### Sandbox lifecycle (the tricky part)\n\n`SANDBOX_BACKENDS` (in `agent/utils/sandbox_state.py`) is an in-process dict keyed by `thread_id`. Thread metadata persists `sandbox_id` across processes. `ensure_sandbox_for_thread` handles four cases:\n\n1. Sandbox cached in memory → ping it (`echo ok`); recreate on `SandboxClientError`. Healthy reused sandboxes also get a GitHub-proxy refresh (recreate on failure).\n2. Metadata says `__creating__` and no cache → reset stale metadata so a fresh sandbox can be created.\n3. No sandbox at all → set `__creating__` sentinel, create one, persist the real id.\n4. Metadata has an id but no cache → reconnect; fall back to recreate on failure.\n\nFor `SANDBOX_TYPE=langsmith` (default), every sandbox creation/refresh also calls `_configure_github_proxy` with a fresh GitHub App installation token (`get_github_app_installation_token`). The proxy injects Basic auth for `github.com` git traffic and Bearer auth for `api.github.com` so sandbox commands can use `GH_TOKEN=dummy gh ...` without storing real tokens in the sandbox. Other providers (modal, daytona, runloop, local) skip the proxy step. Provider is selected via `SANDBOX_TYPE`; factory is `agent/utils/sandbox.py:create_sandbox` (`SANDBOX_FACTORIES` maps each provider name to a creator in `agent/integrations/`).\n\nEvery run re-applies `git config --global user.name/email` for the bot identity, because reused/reconnected sandboxes can lose `--global` config and Vercel preview deploys reject commits whose author email doesn't resolve to a GitHub account.\n\n### Middleware stack (order matters)\n\nConfigured in `agent/server.py:get_agent`, runs around every model call (in this order):\n\n1. `SanitizeToolInputsMiddleware` — strips/normalizes tool inputs before they reach tools.\n2. `ModelCallLimitMiddleware` (from `langchain.agents.middleware`) — caps model calls at `MODEL_CALL_RECURSION_LIMIT` (~half of `DEFAULT_RECURSION_LIMIT`); `exit_behavior=\"end\"`.\n3. `ToolErrorMiddleware` — catches tool exceptions and surfaces them as tool messages.\n4. `SubdirAgentsReadMiddleware` — appends applicable ancestor `AGENTS.md` instructions to `read_file` results once per run, so scoped rules are visible before edits.\n5. `check_message_queue_before_model` — pulls Linear comments / Slack messages that arrived mid-run from the thread queue and injects them as user messages before the next LLM call. This is what makes \"message the agent while it's working\" work.\n6. `SlackAssistantStatusMiddleware` — keeps the Slack \"assistant is typing\"-style status up to date around model calls.\n7. `ensure_no_empty_msg` — after-model hook; when the model emits a message with no tool call (and hasn't already messaged the user or confirmed completion) it re-injects a synthetic `no_op` / `confirming_completion` tool call so the run continues instead of ending prematurely.\n8. `notify_step_limit_reached` — after-agent hook that posts a Slack reply when the agent hits the step limit, so the user gets a clear signal instead of silence.\n9. `SandboxCircuitBreakerMiddleware` — trips the agent out of repeated sandbox failures instead of looping.\n10. `ModelFallbackMiddleware` (optional) — added only when `LLM_FALLBACK_MODEL_ID` or the per-model default fallback differs from the primary model.\n11. `SanitizeThinkingBlocksMiddleware` — strips malformed empty Anthropic thinking blocks immediately before provider calls.\n\nThe system prompt instructs the agent to call a tool every turn, and `ensure_no_empty_msg` re-injects a tool call when it doesn't — together these keep runs from stopping partway through a task.\n\nOther middleware exists in `agent/middleware/` (`ExcludeToolsMiddleware`) but isn't wired into the default agent. The reviewer uses a leaner stack: `SanitizeToolInputsMiddleware`, `ModelCallLimitMiddleware`, `ToolErrorMiddleware`, `SlackAssistantStatusMiddleware`, `SanitizeThinkingBlocksMiddleware`.\n\nThere is intentionally no after-agent safety net that opens a PR for the agent. The agent itself is responsible for committing, pushing, opening/updating the draft PR, and replying in the source channel — all via `GH_TOKEN=dummy gh` and `slack_thread_reply` / `linear_comment`.\n\n### Tools\n\nAll tools live in `agent/tools/` and are flat-imported via `agent/tools/__init__.py`. The set is intentionally small and curated — see README \"Tools — Curated, Not Accumulated\".\n\nWired into `get_agent`:\n`http_request`, `fetch_url`, `web_search`, `linear_comment`, `linear_create_issue`, `linear_delete_issue`, `linear_get_issue`, `linear_get_issue_comments`, `linear_list_teams`, `linear_update_issue`, `request_pr_review`, `schedule_thread_wakeup`, `slack_add_reaction`, `slack_read_thread_messages`, `slack_thread_reply`.\n\nReviewer-only tools (in `agent/reviewer.py`): `add_finding`, `update_finding`, `list_findings`, `publish_review`. The review-style analyzer uses `save_review_style` (exported as `save_review_style_prompt`).\n\nBuilt-in deepagents tools (`read_file`, `write_file`, `edit_file`, `ls`, `glob`, `grep`, `execute`, `write_todos`, `task` for subagent spawning, …) are added by `create_deep_agent` itself; don't duplicate them.\n\n### Models, profiles, and team defaults\n\nModel + reasoning effort are resolved per run in this precedence (highest wins):\n\n1. Per-thread config (`agent_model_id` + `agent_effort` in `configurable`) — set by webhooks/UI.\n2. Per-user dashboard profile override (`agent/dashboard/agent_overrides.py:load_profile`), keyed by resolved GitHub login.\n3. Team default model (`agent/dashboard/team_settings.py:get_team_default_model(\"agent\")`).\n\nSupported model IDs and per-model effort/reasoning rules live in `agent/dashboard/options.py`. Profile flags also drive run behavior — e.g. `profile_create_prs` enables the opt-in Always Create PRs policy. Model construction goes through `agent/utils/model.py` (`make_model`, `provider_model_kwargs`, `fallback_model_id_for`).\n\n### Auth\n\n- **GitHub**: dual-mode. User OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest in the dashboard OAuth store and cached only in process during a run (`utils/auth.py:resolve_github_token`, `utils/github_token.py`). When no user token is available, falls back to a GitHub App installation token (`utils/github_app.py`). The installation token is also what configures the LangSmith sandbox's GitHub proxy.\n- **Webhooks**: GitHub signatures verified in `utils/github_comments.py:verify_github_signature`; Slack/Linear handled in their respective utils.\n- **Dashboard / UI**: GitHub OAuth login lives in `agent/dashboard/oauth.py` and `routes.py` (`/auth/login`, `/auth/callback`, `/auth/logout`, `/me`).\n\n### Thread-id derivation\n\nWebhooks compute deterministic thread ids so the same Linear issue / Slack thread / PR routes back to the same running agent. See `utils/github_comments.py:get_thread_id_from_branch` and the equivalents in `utils/linear.py` / `utils/slack.py`. Reviewer threads have their own deterministic ids and are tagged with `REVIEWER_THREAD_KIND` metadata so the FastAPI side can find them.\n\n## Conventions\n\n- Tests are unit-only by default (`tests/`). Integration tests would go under `tests/integration_tests/` (currently empty — `make integration_tests` no-ops if missing).\n- New sandbox providers: add a module under `agent/integrations/` and wire it into `SANDBOX_FACTORIES` in `agent/utils/sandbox.py`. See `CUSTOMIZATION.md`.\n- New tools: add to `agent/tools/`, export from `agent/tools/__init__.py`, add to the `tools=[...]` list in `server.py:get_agent` (or `reviewer.py` for reviewer-only tools).\n- New middleware: add to `agent/middleware/`, export from `agent/middleware/__init__.py`, add to the `middleware=[...]` list in `server.py:get_agent` — order is significant (see the stack above).\n- Async-only: this app runs exclusively async, so do not add sync/async dual implementations. Implement only the async variant (`awrap_*`, `_arun`, etc.); the sync counterpart is never invoked. Omit the sync method entirely when the interface allows it (e.g. `AgentMiddleware` already raises `NotImplementedError` on the sync path). Only when a type/ABC requires the sync method to exist (e.g. `BaseTool._run` is abstract), define it with a bare `raise NotImplementedError` rather than a real sync implementation.\n- New dashboard endpoints: add to `agent/dashboard/routes.py`. The router is auto-mounted on the FastAPI app.\n- New graphs: register the entrypoint in `langgraph.json` under `graphs`.\n- Minimal-to-no code comments — only when the *why* isn't obvious from the code.\n\nSummary: None\n\n\nTitle: Llms\nURL: https://context7.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/llms.txt\nID: https://context7.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/llms.txt\nScore: None\nPublished Date: None\nAuthor: None\nImage: None\nFavicon: None\nExtras: None\nSubpages: None\nCrawl Date: None\nText: ### Clone and Install Open SWE\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nClone the Open SWE repository and set up the project environment using uv for package management.\n\n```bash\ngit clone https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe.git\ncd open-swe\nuv venv\nsource .venv/bin/activate\nuv sync --all-extras\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Start ngrok for Local Development\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nStart ngrok to expose local webhook endpoints to the internet. Use the provided HTTPS URL in subsequent configurations.\n\n```bash\nngrok http 2024 --url https://some-url-you-configure.ngrok.dev\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Example Default Prompt Markdown\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nIllustrates the format and content for a default prompt markdown file, including default repository and organization conventions.\n\n```markdown\n# Default Prompt\n\n## Default Repository\n\nWhen no repository is specified, work on the **my-app** repository under **my-org**.\n\n## Organization Conventions\n\n- Use conventional commits: feat:, fix:, chore:\n- Always tag the requesting user when work is complete\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Start LangGraph Server\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nRun the LangGraph server using 'uv' for development. The '--no-browser' flag prevents the automatic opening of a web browser.\n\n```bash\nuv run langgraph dev --no-browser\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Registering a New Sandbox Provider Factory\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nExample of how to register a new sandbox provider factory in `agent/utils/sandbox.py`. Import your factory function and add it to the `SANDBOX_FACTORIES` dictionary.\n\n```python\nfrom agent.integrations.my_provider import create_my_provider_sandbox\n\nSANDBOX_FACTORIES = {\n    ...\n    \"my_provider\": create_my_provider_sandbox,\n}\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Use Pre-configured Langchain Model Instance\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nFor full control, pass a pre-configured model instance directly to create_deep_agent. This example shows how to use ChatAnthropic.\n\n```python\nfrom langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic\n\nmodel = ChatAnthropic(model_name=\"claude-sonnet-4-6\", temperature=0, max_tokens=16_000)\n\nreturn create_deep_agent(\n    model=model,\n    ...)\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Retrieve GitHub App Installation ID\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nExtract the Installation ID from the GitHub App installation URL. This ID is required for configuring the app.\n\n```bash\nhttps://github.com/settings/installations/12345678\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Process Trigger Event and Start Agent Run\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nCreate a function to process incoming trigger events, generate a deterministic ID, and initiate an agent run with specific configuration.\n\n```python\nfrom langgraph.client import LangGraphClient\nfrom agent.utils import generate_deterministic_id, get_client\n\nLANGGRAPH_URL = \"http://localhost:8080\" # Example URL\n\nasync def process_my_trigger(task_description: str, repo_config: dict):\n    thread_id = generate_deterministic_id(task_description)\n    langgraph_client = get_client(url=LANGGRAPH_URL)\n    \n    await langgraph_client.runs.create(\n        thread_id,\n        \"agent\",\n        input={\"messages\": [{\"role\": \"user\", \"content\": task_description}]},\n        config={\"configurable\": {\n            \"repo\": repo_config,\n            \"source\": \"my-trigger\",\n            \"user_email\": \"user@example.com\",\n        }},\n        if_not_exists=\"create\",\n    )\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Environment Variables Configuration\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nThis is a comprehensive list of environment variables for configuring Open SWE. Fill in the variables relevant to your setup, including API keys and secrets.\n\n```bash\n# === LangSmith ===\nLANGSMITH_API_KEY_PROD=\"\"\nLANGCHAIN_TRACING_V2=\"true\"\nLANGCHAIN_PROJECT=\"\"\nLANGSMITH_TENANT_ID_PROD=\"\"\nLANGSMITH_TRACING_PROJECT_ID_PROD=\"\"\nLANGSMITH_URL_PROD=\"https://smith.langchain.com\"\n\n# === LLM ===\nANTHROPIC_API_KEY=\"\"\n\n# === GitHub App (required) ===\nGITHUB_APP_ID=\"\"\nGITHUB_APP_PRIVATE_KEY=\"-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\n...\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\"\nGITHUB_APP_INSTALLATION_ID=\"\"\n\n# === GitHub Webhook (required) ===\nGITHUB_WEBHOOK_SECRET=\"\"\n\n# === GitHub OAuth via LangSmith (optional) ===\n# Without these, all operations use the GitHub App's bot token.\n# With these, each user authenticates with their own GitHub account.\nGITHUB_OAUTH_PROVIDER_ID=\"\"\n\n# === Repo Allowlist (optional) ===\n# Comma-separated list of GitHub orgs the agent is allowed to operate on.\n# Leave empty to allow all orgs.\nALLOWED_GITHUB_ORGS=\"\"\n# Comma-separated list of specific owner/repo pairs the agent is allowed to operate on.\n# For GitHub/Linear webhooks, a repo is allowed if its org is in ALLOWED_GITHUB_ORGS OR its owner/repo is in ALLOWED_GITHUB_REPOS.\n# Slack mentions are not rejected from regex-inferred repository text; repository access is bounded by GitHub App installation permissions.\n# Leave both empty to allow all repos.\nALLOWED_GITHUB_REPOS=\"\"\n\n# === Default Repository ===\n# Used across all triggers when no repo is specified.\nDEFAULT_REPO_OWNER=\"\"\nDEFAULT_REPO_NAME=\"\"\n\n# === Linear (if using Linear trigger) ===\nLINEAR_API_KEY=\"\"\nLINEAR_WEBHOOK_SECRET=\"\"\n\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Re-review Prompt Context Structure\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/REVIEWER_DESIGN.md\n\nDefines the structured message sent to the agent when a new commit is pushed to a previously reviewed PR. It includes PR details, previous findings, and the diff since the last review, guiding the agent on how to handle existing findings and identify new issues.\n\n```text\nA new commit has been pushed to the PR you previously reviewed.\n\nPR: <owner>/<repo>#<number>\nPrevious reviewed SHA: <last_reviewed_sha>\nNew HEAD SHA: <new_sha>\n\nExisting findings:\n- [<id>] (<severity>, <category>) <file>:<line> — <description>  [status: <status>]\n- ...\n\nDiff since previous reviewed SHA:\n<diff>\n\nFor each existing open finding, decide whether the new commits:\n  - resolved it (mark resolved with update_finding(status=\"resolved\"))\n  - left it unchanged (no action)\n  - changed it materially (update via update_finding with a note, or update + revised suggestion, or close + add_finding)\n\nThen review the new diff for any net-new issues and add them with add_finding.\nFinally call publish_review to post inline comments + suggestions for the new findings, and resolve the GitHub threads for findings that just moved to resolved.\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Project Commands with Make\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/AGENTS.md\n\nCommon commands for managing dependencies, development server, testing, and linting/formatting using Make, uv, and ruff.\n\n```bash\nmake install            # uv pip install -e .\nmake dev                # langgraph dev — run the LangGraph dev server (graph defined in langgraph.json)\nmake run                # uvicorn agent.webapp:app --reload --port 8000 (webhook server only)\nmake test               # uv run pytest -vvv tests/\nmake test TEST_FILE=tests/test_open_pr_middleware.py    # single test file\nuv run pytest -vvv tests/test_open_pr_middleware.py::test_name  # single test\nmake lint               # ruff check + ruff format --diff\nmake format             # ruff format + ruff check --fix\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Create Sandbox Snapshot using Helper Script\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nUses a helper script to create a sandbox snapshot. Specify the name and Docker image for the snapshot.\n\n```bash\nuv run python scripts/create_sandbox_snapshot.py \\\n  --name open-swe-gh-cli-amd64 \\\n  --image johanneslangchain/open-swe-sandbox:gh-cli-amd64\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Build and Push Docker Image\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nBuilds a Docker image for the sandbox environment and pushes it to a registry. Use `--platform` to specify the target architecture, especially for Apple Silicon.\n\n```bash\ndocker buildx build \\\n  --platform linux/amd64 \\\n  -t <your-docker-hub>/<name-of-your-image> \\\n  --push .\n```\n\n```bash\ndocker buildx build \\\n  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \\\n  -t <your-docker-hub>/<name-of-your-image> \\\n  --push .\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Open-SWE Configuration File\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nThe 'langgraph.json' file defines the graph entry point and HTTP application for production deployment. Ensure this file is present at the project root.\n\n```json\n{\n  \"graphs\": {\n    \"agent\": \"agent.server:get_agent\"\n  },\n  \"http\": {\n    \"app\": \"agent.webapp:app\"\n  }\n}\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Deterministic Repo Preparation\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/REVIEWER_DESIGN.md\n\nEnsures the repository is in a consistent state before the agent's first model call. It clones the repo if it doesn't exist or fetches and checks out the target commit if it does.\n\n```bash\nif /workspace/<repo> exists:\n    git fetch && git checkout <target_sha>\nelse:\n    gh repo clone <owner>/<repo> /workspace/<repo> && git checkout <target_sha>\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Add shadcn/ui Component\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/ui/README.md\n\nRun this command to add a new shadcn/ui component to your project. Components are placed in the `components` directory.\n\n```bash\nnpx shadcn@latest add button\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Slack App Manifest Configuration\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nUse this JSON manifest to create a Slack app. Replace placeholder URLs with your specific provider ID and ngrok URL.\n\n```json\n{\n    \"display_information\": {\n        \"name\": \"Open SWE\",\n        \"description\": \"Enables Open SWE to interact with your workspace\",\n        \"background_color\": \"#000000\"\n    },\n    \"features\": {\n        \"app_home\": {\n            \"home_tab_enabled\": false,\n            \"messages_tab_enabled\": true,\n            \"messages_tab_read_only_enabled\": false\n        },\n        \"bot_user\": {\n            \"display_name\": \"Open SWE\",\n            \"always_online\": true\n        }\n    },\n    \"oauth_config\": {\n        \"redirect_urls\": [\n            \"https://smith.langchain.com/host-oauth-callback/<your-provider-id>\"\n        ],\n        \"scopes\": {\n            \"bot\": [\n                \"reactions:write\",\n                \"app_mentions:read\",\n                \"channels:history\",\n                \"channels:read\",\n                \"chat:write\",\n                \"groups:history\",\n                \"groups:read\",\n                \"im:history\",\n                \"im:read\",\n                \"im:write\",\n                \"mpim:history\",\n                \"mpim:read\",\n                \"team:read\",\n                \"users:read\",\n                \"users:read.email\"\n            ]\n        }\n    },\n    \"settings\": {\n        \"event_subscriptions\": {\n            \"request_url\": \"https://<your-ngrok-url>/webhooks/slack\",\n            \"bot_events\": [\n                \"app_mention\",\n                \"message.im\",\n                \"message.mpim\"\n            ]\n        },\n        \"org_deploy_enabled\": false,\n        \"socket_mode_enabled\": false,\n        \"token_rotation_enabled\": false\n    }\n}\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Register Datadog Search Tool\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nRegister a new tool by importing it and adding it to the tools list in `agent/server.py`. The tool's docstring serves as its description.\n\n```python\nfrom .tools.datadog_search import datadog_search\n\nreturn create_deep_agent(\n    ...\n    tools=[\n        http_request, fetch_url,\n        linear_comment, slack_thread_reply,\n        datadog_search,  # new tool\n    ],\n    ...\n)\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Create LangSmith Sandbox Snapshot\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nCreates a sandbox snapshot in LangSmith using the SDK. Requires an API key and specifies the Docker image and filesystem capacity.\n\n```python\nfrom langsmith.sandbox import SandboxClient\n\nclient = SandboxClient(api_key=\"<your key>\")\nsnapshot = client.create_snapshot(\n    name=\"open-swe\",\n    docker_image=\"johanneslangchain/open-swe-sandbox:gh-cli-amd64\",  # built from ./Dockerfile\n    fs_capacity_bytes=32 * 1024**3,\n)\nprint(snapshot.id)\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Switch Language Models using make_model\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nUse the make_model helper function to easily configure different language models. Specify the provider and model name, along with parameters like temperature and max_tokens.\n\n```python\n# Anthropic\nmodel=make_model(\"anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6\", temperature=0, max_tokens=16_000)\n\n# OpenAI (uses Responses API by default)\nmodel=make_model(\"openai:gpt-5.5\", max_tokens=128_000, reasoning={\"effort\": \"medium\"})\n\n# Google\nmodel=make_model(\"google_genai:gemini-2.5-pro\", temperature=0, max_tokens=16_000)\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Environment Variables for Open-SWE\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nConfigure these environment variables to enable integrations with Slack, Exa, and sandbox environments. Ensure secrets like API keys and tokens are kept secure.\n\n```bash\nSLACK_BOT_TOKEN=\"\"\nSLACK_BOT_USER_ID=\"\"\nSLACK_BOT_USERNAME=\"\"\nSLACK_SIGNING_SECRET=\"\"\n\nEXA_API_KEY=\"\"\n\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_SNAPSHOT_ID=\"\"\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_SNAPSHOT_FS_CAPACITY_BYTES=\"\"\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_VCPUS=\"\"\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_MEM_BYTES=\"\"\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_IDLE_TTL_SECONDS=\"\"\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_DELETE_AFTER_STOP_SECONDS=\"\"\n\nTOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY=\"\"\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Core Agent Assembly in `agent/server.py`\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nThis snippet shows the key lines in `agent/server.py` for assembling the core agent, including model configuration, system prompt construction, tool selection, sandbox backend, and middleware.\n\n```python\nmodel_id = os.environ.get(\"LLM_MODEL_ID\", DEFAULT_LLM_MODEL_ID)\nmodel_kwargs = {\"max_tokens\": DEFAULT_LLM_MAX_TOKENS}\nif model_id == DEFAULT_LLM_MODEL_ID:\n    model_kwargs[\"reasoning\"] = DEFAULT_LLM_REASONING\n\nreturn create_deep_agent(\n    model=make_model(model_id, **model_kwargs),\n    system_prompt=construct_system_prompt(...),\n    tools=[http_request, fetch_url, linear_comment, slack_thread_reply],\n    backend=sandbox_backend,\n    middleware=[\n        ToolErrorMiddleware(),\n        check_message_queue_before_model,\n        ensure_no_empty_msg,\n        notify_step_limit_reached,\n    ],\n)\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Create Deep Agent Configuration\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/README.md\n\nConfigure a Deep Agent with a model, system prompt, tools, backend, and middleware. Ensure necessary imports for tools and middleware are included.\n\n```python\ncreate_deep_agent(\n    model=\"openai:gpt-5.5\",\n    system_prompt=construct_system_prompt(...),\n    tools=[http_request, fetch_url, linear_comment, slack_thread_reply],\n    backend=sandbox_backend,\n    middleware=[ToolErrorMiddleware(), check_message_queue_before_model, ...],\n)\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Configure Sandbox Environment Variables\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nSets environment variables to configure sandbox behavior, including snapshot ID, filesystem capacity, CPU, memory, and idle/delete timeouts.\n\n```bash\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_SNAPSHOT_ID=\"<snapshot-uuid>\"\n# Optional; overrides the snapshot's root FS size at sandbox boot. Default is 32 GiB.\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_SNAPSHOT_FS_CAPACITY_BYTES=\"34359738368\"\n# Optional; number of vCPUs per sandbox. Default is 4.\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_VCPUS=\"4\"\n# Optional; memory in bytes per sandbox. Default is 15 GiB.\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_MEM_BYTES=\"16106127360\"\n# Optional; auto-stop a sandbox after this many seconds of inactivity. Default is 600 (10 min). 0 disables.\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_IDLE_TTL_SECONDS=\"600\"\n# Optional; delete a stopped sandbox after this many seconds. Default is 86400 (24 hours). 0 disables.\nDEFAULT_SANDBOX_DELETE_AFTER_STOP_SECONDS=\"86400\"\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Implement Custom Sandbox Backend\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nExtend BaseSandbox to create a custom sandbox provider. You only need to implement the execute() method, as BaseSandbox handles file operations by delegating to execute().\n\n```python\nfrom deepagents.backends.sandbox import BaseSandbox\nfrom deepagents.backends.protocol import ExecuteResponse\n\nclass MySandbox(BaseSandbox):\n    def __init__(self, connection):\n        self._conn = connection\n\n    @property\n    def id(self) -> str:\n        return self._conn.id\n\n    def execute(self, command: str, *, timeout: int | None = None) -> ExecuteResponse:\n        result = self._conn.run(command, timeout=timeout or 300)\n        return ExecuteResponse(\n            output=result.stdout + result.stderr,\n            exit_code=result.exit_code,\n            truncated=False,\n        )\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Configure Allowed GitHub Repositories\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nSpecify which GitHub organizations or specific repositories the agent is allowed to operate on. Leave empty to allow all repositories.\n\n```bash\n# Allow all repos in these orgs\nALLOWED_GITHUB_ORGS=\"langchain-ai,anthropics\"\n\n# Allow specific repos (owner/repo format)\nALLOWED_GITHUB_REPOS=\"some-user/their-repo,another-org/specific-repo\"\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Route to Different Models Based on Context\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nDynamically select language models within the get_agent function based on configuration parameters like the 'source' from the RunnableConfig.\n\n```python\nasync def get_agent(config: RunnableConfig) -> Pregel:\n    source = config[\"configurable\"].get(\"source\")\n    \n    if source == \"slack\":\n        # Faster model for Slack Q&A\n        model = make_model(\"anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6\", temperature=0, max_tokens=16_000)\n    else:\n        # Full model for code changes from Linear\n        model = make_model(\"openai:gpt-5.5\", max_tokens=128_000, reasoning={\"effort\": \"medium\"})\n    \n    return create_deep_agent(model=model, ...)\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Configure Linear Team to Repository Mapping\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nMap Linear teams and projects to specific GitHub repositories. This helps Open SWE route issues to the correct repository.\n\n```python\nLINEAR_TEAM_TO_REPO = {\n    \"My Team\": {\"owner\": \"my-org\", \"name\": \"my-repo\"},\n    \"Engineering\": {\n        \"projects\": {\n            \"backend\": {\"owner\": \"my-org\", \"name\": \"backend\"},\n            \"frontend\": {\"owner\": \"my-org\", \"name\": \"frontend\"},\n        },\n        \"default\": {\"owner\": \"my-org\", \"name\": \"monorepo\"},\n    },\n}\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Add a Custom Tool\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nTo add a new tool, create a Python file in agent/tools/, define a function for the tool's logic, and then add it to the tools list used by the agent.\n\n```python\n# Example - adding a Datadog search tool:\n\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Run Evaluation (Limited)\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/evals/reviewer/README.md\n\nRun a limited evaluation of the reviewer graph with only 3 PRs. This is useful for quick smoke tests.\n\n```bash\nuv run python -m evals.reviewer.run_eval --limit 3\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Build Dataset (Dry Run)\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/evals/reviewer/README.md\n\nPerform a dry run of the dataset build process to generate a JSON file without uploading it to LangSmith. This is useful for testing and verification.\n\n```bash\nuv run python -m evals.reviewer.build_dataset --dry-run\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Build Dataset (Upload)\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/evals/reviewer/README.md\n\nBuild and upload the evaluation dataset to LangSmith. This command resolves SHAs using `gh` and creates a dataset named 'openswe-reviewer-v1'.\n\n```bash\nuv run python -m evals.reviewer.build_dataset --dataset-name openswe-reviewer-v1\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Run Evaluation\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/evals/reviewer/README.md\n\nExecute the evaluation of the reviewer graph. This command runs the full evaluation process.\n\n```bash\nuv run python -m evals.reviewer.run_eval\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Environment Variables\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nConfiguration variables required for different functionalities of the OpenSWE project.\n\n```APIDOC\n## Environment Variables\n\n### Slack Integration\n- `SLACK_BOT_TOKEN`: Slack bot token.\n- `SLACK_BOT_USER_ID`: Slack bot user ID.\n- `SLACK_BOT_USERNAME`: Slack bot username.\n- `SLACK_SIGNING_SECRET`: Slack signing secret.\n\n### Exa Search Tool (Optional)\n- `EXA_API_KEY`: API key for the Exa web search tool.\n\n### Sandbox Environment (Optional)\n- `DEFAULT_SANDBOX_SNAPSHOT_ID`: Snapshot ID for the sandbox.\n- `DEFAULT_SANDBOX_SNAPSHOT_FS_CAPACITY_BYTES`: Root filesystem capacity in bytes.\n- `DEFAULT_SANDBOX_VCPUS`: Number of vCPUs per sandbox.\n- `DEFAULT_SANDBOX_MEM_BYTES`: Memory in bytes per sandbox.\n- `DEFAULT_SANDBOX_IDLE_TTL_SECONDS`: Time in seconds before an idle sandbox is auto-stopped.\n- `DEFAULT_SANDBOX_DELETE_AFTER_STOP_SECONDS`: Time in seconds after which a sandbox is deleted after stopping.\n\n### Token Encryption\n- `TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY`: Key for token encryption. Supports Fernet keys or a comma/newline-separated ordered list of keys (most recent first) for rotation.\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Import shadcn/ui Component\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/ui/README.md\n\nImport UI components into your React components using this syntax. Ensure the path matches your project structure.\n\n```tsx\nimport { Button } from \"@/components/ui/button\";\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Define Communication Tool for Agent Replies\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nImplement a communication tool function to allow the agent to send replies back to the triggering service. This is an optional step.\n\n```python\n# agent/tools/my_trigger_reply.py\ndef my_trigger_reply(message: str) -> dict:\n    \"\"\"Post a reply to the triggering service.\"\"\"\n    # Your API call here\n    ...\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Override Default Prompt Path\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nConfigure a custom path for the default prompt file by setting the DEFAULT_PROMPT_PATH environment variable.\n\n```bash\nDEFAULT_PROMPT_PATH=\"/path/to/my-org-prompt.md\"\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Rotating TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nInstructions on how to rotate the `TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY` to enhance security and manage key updates.\n\n```APIDOC\n## Rotating TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY\n\n`TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY` accepts either a single Fernet key or a comma- or\nnewline-separated **ordered list of keys, most-recent-first**. New writes always\nencrypt under the first key; reads try every key in order. To rotate without\ninvalidating already-stored GitHub tokens:\n\n1. Generate a new key: `openssl rand -base64 32`.\n2. Prepend it to `TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY`, keeping the old key second:\n   ```\n TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY=\"<new_key>,<old_key>\"\n   ```\n   Restart the server. New encryptions use `<new_key>`; existing ciphertexts\n   still decrypt against `<old_key>`.\n3. Let active threads cycle (each fresh OAuth flow re-encrypts under the new\n   key). After every active thread has re-authed, drop the old key:\n   ```\n TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY=\"<new_key>\"\n   ```\n   Any thread still holding ciphertext under `<old_key>` will fail to decrypt\n   and the user will be re-prompted to authenticate — same UX as if the thread\n   had never authed.\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Add Finding Tool\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/REVIEWER_DESIGN.md\n\nTool to add a new code finding to the thread state. Supports single-line, multi-line, and file-level findings. The `suggestion` parameter must be an exact replacement for the specified lines.\n\n```python\nadd_finding(severity, category, file, start_line, end_line, description, suggestion=None, side=\"RIGHT\") -> id\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Map Linear Teams to GitHub Repositories\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nDefine a mapping from Linear teams and projects to specific GitHub repositories. This configuration is used to route issues to the correct repository when not explicitly specified in a comment.\n\n```python\nLINEAR_TEAM_TO_REPO = {\n    \"Engineering\": {\n        \"projects\": {\n            \"backend\": {\"owner\": \"my-org\", \"name\": \"backend\"},\n            \"frontend\": {\"owner\": \"my-org\", \"name\": \"frontend\"},\n        },\n        \"default\": {\"owner\": \"my-org\", \"name\": \"monorepo\"},\n    },\n}\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Set Default LLM Model via Environment Variable\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nConfigure the default language model for the agent by setting the LLM_MODEL_ID environment variable. This uses the provider:model format.\n\n```bash\n# Set the model via environment variable (uses provider:model format)\nLLM_MODEL_ID=\"anthropic:claude-sonnet-4-6\"\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Integrate Custom Middleware into Agent Configuration\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nAppend your custom middleware function to the existing middleware list within the `get_agent()` configuration. This ensures your custom logic runs as part of the agent's execution loop.\n\n```python\nmiddleware=[\n    ToolErrorMiddleware(),\n    check_message_queue_before_model,\n    ensure_no_empty_msg,\n    notify_step_limit_reached,\n    run_ci_check,  # new middleware\n]\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Configure GitHub User Access\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/INSTALLATION.md\n\nMap GitHub usernames to email addresses to control agent access. This is used to filter which users can trigger the agent.\n\n```python\nGITHUB_USER_EMAIL_MAP = {\n    \"their-github-username\": \"their-email@example.com\",\n}\n```\n\n--------------------------------\n\n### Conditional Toolset Configuration\n\nSource: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/blob/main/CUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nDynamically configure the toolset based on the source of the trigger. These agents are connected to internal systems with the right context, permissioning, and safety boundaries to operate with minimal human oversight.\n\nOpen SWE is the open-source version of this pattern. Built on [LangGraph](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/) and [Deep Agents](https://github.com/langchain-ai/deepagents), it gives you the same architecture those companies built internally: cloud sandboxes, Slack and Linear invocation, subagent orchestration, and automatic PR creation — ready to customize for your own codebase and workflows.\n\n> [!NOTE]\n> 💬 Read the **announcement blog post [here](https://blog.langchain.com/open-swe-an-open-source-framework-for-internal-coding-agents/)**\n\n---\n\n## Architecture\n\nOpen SWE makes the same core architectural decisions as the best internal coding agents. Here's how it maps to the patterns described in [this overview](https://x.com/kishan_dahya/status/2028971339974099317) of Stripe's Minions, Ramp's Inspect, and Coinbase's Cloudbot:\n\n### 1. Agent Harness — Composed on Deep Agents\n\nRather than forking an existing agent or building from scratch, Open SWE **composes** on the [Deep Agents](https://github.com/langchain-ai/deepagents) framework — similar to how Ramp built on top of OpenCode. This gives you an upgrade path (pull in upstream improvements) while letting you customize the orchestration, tools, and middleware for your org.\n\n```python\ncreate_deep_agent(\n    model=\"openai:gpt-5.5\",\n    system_prompt=construct_system_prompt(...),\n    tools=[http_request, fetch_url, linear_comment, slack_thread_reply],\n    backend=sandbox_backend,\n    middleware=[ToolErrorMiddleware(), check_message_queue_before_model, ...],\n)\n```\n\n### 2. Sandbox — Isolated Cloud Environments\n\nEvery task runs in its own **isolated cloud sandbox** — a remote Linux environment with full shell access. The repo is cloned in, the agent gets full permissions, and the blast radius of any mistake is fully contained. No production access, no confirmation prompts.\n\nOpen SWE supports multiple sandbox providers out of the box — [Modal](https://modal.com/), [Daytona](https://www.daytona.io/), [Runloop](https://www.runloop.ai/), [E2B](https://e2b.dev/), and [LangSmith](https://smith.langchain.com/) — and you can plug in your own. See the [Customization Guide](CUSTOMIZATION.md#1-sandbox) for details.\n\nThis follows the principle all three companies converge on: **isolate first, then give full permissions inside the boundary.**\n\n- Each thread gets a persistent sandbox (reused across follow-up messages)\n- Sandboxes auto-recreate if they become unreachable\n- Multiple tasks run in parallel — each in its own sandbox, no queuing\n\n### 3. Tools — Curated, Not Accumulated\n\nStripe's key insight: *tool curation matters more than tool quantity.* Open SWE follows this principle with a small, focused toolset:\n\n| Tool | Purpose |\n| --- | --- |\n| `execute` | Shell commands in the sandbox |\n| `fetch_url` | Fetch web pages as markdown |\n| `http_request` | API calls (GET, POST, etc.) |\n| `linear_comment` | Post updates to Linear tickets |\n| `slack_add_reaction` | React to Slack messages |\n| `slack_thread_reply` | Reply in Slack threads |\n\nGitHub operations are performed with `GH_TOKEN=dummy gh` inside the sandbox, backed by the LangSmith proxy. Plus the built-in Deep Agents tools: `read_file`, `write_file`, `edit_file`, `ls`, `glob`, `grep`, `write_todos`, and `task` (subagent spawning).\n\n**Optional observability tools (server-side):** Admins can connect Datadog and LangSmith from team settings (Admin → Observability credentials). When connected, the agent gains Datadog tools (via Datadog's hosted MCP server, default `toolsets=core`) and read-only LangSmith tools (`langsmith_get_trace`, `langsmith_list_runs`). These run in the LangGraph server process using credentials encrypted at rest — the sandbox never holds Datadog or LangSmith keys. They are loaded **only for runs triggered by an authorized user** (admins, plus any emails in `OBSERVABILITY_AUTHORIZED_EMAILS`), so a prompt-injected run from an untrusted contributor cannot reach team observability data. Use scoped, read-oriented keys regardless: observability data (logs, traces) is attacker-influenced content that can carry prompt injection, and the agent has network egress — the same residual-risk class as `web_search` / `fetch_url`.\n\n**Optional Corridor guardrails (server-side MCP):** Set `CORRIDOR_API_TOKEN` (or `CORRIDOR_MCP_TOKEN` / `CORRIDOR_TOKEN`) to load Corridor's hosted MCP server for each agent run. Open SWE exposes only Corridor's `analyzePlan` tool. `CORRIDOR_MCP_URL` defaults to `https://app.corridor.dev/api/mcp`; if set explicitly, Open SWE only accepts the same HTTPS host and `/api/mcp` path. Tokens are sent via `Authorization: Bearer ...` from the LangGraph server process and are never placed in the sandbox. A legacy `?token=...` URL is accepted and normalized into the header form.\n\n### 4. Context Engineering — AGENTS.md + Source Context\n\nOpen SWE gathers context from two sources:\n\n- **`AGENTS.md`** — If the repo contains an `AGENTS.md` file at the root, it's read from the sandbox and injected into the system prompt. This is your repo-level equivalent of Stripe's rule files: encoding conventions, testing requirements, and architectural decisions that every agent run should follow.\n- **Source context** — The full Linear issue (title, description, comments) or Slack thread history is assembled and passed to the agent, so it starts with rich context rather than discovering everything through tool calls.\n\n### 5. Orchestration — Subagents + Middleware\n\nOpen SWE's orchestration has two layers:\n\n**Subagents:** The Deep Agents framework natively supports spawning child agents via the `task` tool. The main agent can fan out independent subtasks to isolated subagents — each with its own middleware stack, todo list, and file operations. This is similar to Ramp's child sessions for parallel work.\n\n**Middleware:** Deterministic middleware hooks run around the agent loop:\n\n- **`check_message_queue_before_model`** — Injects follow-up messages (Linear comments or Slack messages that arrive mid-run) before the next model call. You can message the agent while it's working and it'll pick up your input at its next step.\n- **`notify_step_limit_reached`** — After-agent hook that posts a Slack reply when the agent hits the model-call limit, so users get a clear signal instead of silence.\n- **`ToolErrorMiddleware`** — Catches and handles tool errors gracefully.\n\n### 6. Invocation — Slack, Linear, and GitHub\n\nAll three companies in the article converge on **Slack as the primary invocation surface**. Open SWE does the same:\n\n- **Slack** — Mention the bot in any thread. Supports `repo:owner/name` syntax to specify which repo to work on. The agent replies in-thread with status updates and PR links.\n- **Linear** — Comment `@openswe` on any issue. The agent reads the full issue context, reacts with 👀 to acknowledge, and posts results back as comments.\n- **GitHub** — Tag `@openswe` in PR comments on agent-created PRs to have it address review feedback and push fixes to the same branch.\n\nEach invocation creates a deterministic thread ID, so follow-up messages on the same issue or thread route to the same running agent.\n\n### 7. Validation — Prompt-Driven\n\nThe agent is instructed to run linters, formatters, and tests before committing, and is responsible end-to-end for committing, pushing, opening/updating the draft PR, and replying in the source channel.\nThis is an area where you can extend Open SWE for your org: add deterministic CI checks, visual verification, or review gates as additional middleware. See the [Customization Guide](CUSTOMIZATION.md#6-middleware) for how.\n\n---\n\n## Comparison\n\n| Decision | Open SWE | Stripe (Minions) | Ramp (Inspect) | Coinbase (Cloudbot) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| **Harness** | Composed (Deep Agents/LangGraph) | Forked (Goose) | Composed (OpenCode) | Built from scratch |\n| **Sandbox** | Pluggable (Modal, Daytona, Runloop, etc.) | AWS EC2 devboxes (pre-warmed) | Modal containers (pre-warmed) | In-house |\n| **Tools** | ~15, curated | ~500, curated per-agent | OpenCode SDK + extensions | MCPs + custom Skills |\n| **Context** | AGENTS.md + issue/thread | Rule files + pre-hydration | OpenCode built-in | Linear-first + MCPs |\n| **Orchestration** | Subagents + middleware | Blueprints (deterministic + agentic) | Sessions + child sessions | Three modes |\n| **Invocation** | Slack, Linear, GitHub | Slack + embedded buttons | Slack + web + Chrome extension | Slack-native |\n| **Validation** | Prompt-driven | 3-layer (local + CI + 1 retry) | Visual DOM verification | Agent councils + auto-merge |\n\n---\n\n## Features\n\n- **Trigger from Linear, Slack, or GitHub** — mention `@openswe` in a comment to kick off a task\n- **Instant acknowledgement** — reacts with 👀 the moment it picks up your message\n- **Message it while it's running** — send follow-up messages mid-task and it'll pick them up before its next step\n- **Run multiple tasks in parallel** — each task runs in its own isolated cloud sandbox\n- **GitHub OAuth built-in** — authenticates with your GitHub account automatically\n- **Opens PRs automatically** — commits changes and opens a draft PR when done, linked back to your ticket\n- **Subagent support** — the agent can spawn child agents for parallel subtasks\n- **Web dashboard** — a companion app (in `ui/`) for GitHub login, per-user model/profile settings, team defaults, enabled-repo and review-style management, user mappings, and an Agents chat UI\n\n---\n\n## Getting Started\n\n- Guide](CUSTOMIZATION.md)** — swap the sandbox, model, tools, triggers, system prompt, and middleware for your org\n\n## License\n\nMIT\nSummary: None\n\n\nTitle: CLAUDE.md\nURL: None\nExtras: None\nSubpages: None\nCrawl Date: None\nText: # CLAUDE.md\n\n- Branch: main\n- Repository: langchain-ai/open-swe\n\n---\n\n# CLAUDE.md\n\nThis file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.\n\n## Project\n\nOpen SWE is an open-source coding-agent framework built on **LangGraph** + **Deep Agents** (`deepagents.create_deep_agent`). It runs as a LangGraph app: each thread spawns its own isolated cloud sandbox, and the agent is invoked from Slack, Linear, or GitHub (PR comments, plus auto-review on opened / ready-for-review).\n\nA separate **reviewer** graph runs read-only code reviews on PRs, and a **review-style analyzer** graph learns per-repo review style from historical PRs.\n\n## Commands\n\nDependencies are managed with **uv**. Tests use pytest (`asyncio_mode = \"auto\"`). Lint/format is **ruff** (line-length 100, target py311). `requires-python = \">=3.11\"`; `langgraph.json` pins the runtime to 3.12.\n\n```bash\nmake install            # uv pip install -e .\nmake dev                # uv run langgraph dev — serves all three graphs + the FastAPI app from langgraph.json\nmake run                # uvicorn agent.webapp:app --reload --port 8000 (FastAPI only, no LangGraph runtime)\nmake test               # uv run pytest -vvv tests/\nmake test TEST_FILE=tests/test_open_pr_middleware.py    # single test file\nuv run pytest -vvv tests/test_open_pr_middleware.py::test_name  # single test\nmake lint               # ruff check + ruff format --diff\nmake format             # ruff format + ruff check --fix\n```\n\n`langgraph.json` declares three graph entrypoints and the FastAPI app, all served together by `langgraph dev`:\n\n| Graph | Entrypoint | Purpose |\n|---|---|---|\n| `agent` | `agent.server:traced_agent` (wraps `get_agent`) | Main coding agent (Slack/Linear/GitHub-triggered). |\n| `reviewer` | `agent.reviewer:traced_reviewer_agent` (wraps `get_reviewer_agent`) | Read-only PR reviewer. Findings model + `publish_review`. |\n| `analyzer` | `agent.analyzer:traced_analyzer` (wraps `get_analyzer`) | Learns per-repo reviewer style from historical PRs and this reviewer's own finding outcomes. |\n\nThe FastAPI app is `agent.webapp:app`.\n\n## Architecture\n\n### Entrypoints\n\n- **`agent/server.py` → `get_agent(config)`** — main graph factory. Called per-thread. Resolves the GitHub token, gets-or-creates the sandbox for the thread, resolves the team/profile/per-thread model + effort, then constructs a fresh `create_deep_agent(...)` with the curated tool list and middleware stack. The agent itself is stateless — all per-thread state lives in the sandbox + thread metadata.\n- **`agent/reviewer.py` → `get_reviewer_agent(config)`** — reviewer graph factory. Shares `ensure_sandbox_for_thread` with the main agent but wires a reviewer-only toolset (`add_finding`, `update_finding`, `list_findings`, `publish_review`, `web_search`, `fetch_url`, `http_request`) and a different system prompt that pins the single-evolving-findings model and the diff-anchored bar for filing a finding. Read-only: no commit/push/PR-opening tools.\n- **`agent/analyzer.py` → `get_analyzer(config)`** — small graph that emits a per-repo style prompt via the `save_review_style_prompt` tool, consumed by the reviewer as a \"repository-specific review style\" appendix. It runs in one of two modes (`analyzer_mode` in `configurable`): **bootstrap** (cold-start: crawl historical PR reviews) and **continual** (nightly: refine using this reviewer's own finding outcomes via `read_finding_outcomes`). Each mode's procedure lives in a deepagents **skill** (`agent/skills/bootstrap-repo-analysis/`, `agent/skills/continual-learning/`) served as virtual files via a `CompositeBackend` `/skills/` route + `StateBackend` (seeded into the run's `files` channel by the launcher — never written to the sandbox). Launchers and the per-repo nightly cron live in `agent/dashboard/review_style_jobs.py` and `agent/dashboard/analyzer_cron.py`; the cron is registered when bootstrap completes.\n- **`agent/webapp.py`** — custom FastAPI routes mounted alongside the LangGraph server. Webhooks land here (GitHub, Linear, Slack). Each webhook resolves a deterministic `thread_id` (so follow-up messages route to the same agent run) and triggers/streams a run via the `langgraph_sdk` client. Also auto-reviews PRs on `opened` / `ready_for_review` events when the repo+author opt in.\n- **`agent/dashboard/`** — `router` mounted under the FastAPI app at startup (`app.include_router(dashboard_router)`). Owns GitHub OAuth, per-user profiles, admin endpoints, team defaults, enabled-repo lists, review-style management, and the Agents chat thread API used by the UI in `ui/`.\n\n### Sandbox lifecycle (the tricky part)\n\n`SANDBOX_BACKENDS` (in `agent/utils/sandbox_state.py`) is an in-process dict keyed by `thread_id`. Thread metadata persists `sandbox_id` across processes. `ensure_sandbox_for_thread` handles four cases:\n\n1. Sandbox cached in memory → ping it (`echo ok`); recreate on `SandboxClientError`. Healthy reused sandboxes also get a GitHub-proxy refresh (recreate on failure).\n2. Metadata says `__creating__` and no cache → poll until ready (`_wait_for_sandbox_id`).\n3. No sandbox at all → set `__creating__` sentinel, create one, persist the real id.\n4. Metadata has an id but no cache → reconnect; fall back to recreate on failure.\n\nFor `SANDBOX_TYPE=langsmith` (default), every sandbox creation/refresh also calls `_configure_github_proxy` with a fresh GitHub App installation token (`get_github_app_installation_token`). The proxy injects Basic auth for `github.com` git traffic and Bearer auth for `api.github.com` so sandbox commands can use `GH_TOKEN=dummy gh ...` without storing real tokens in the sandbox. Other providers (modal, daytona, runloop, local) skip the proxy step. Provider is selected via `SANDBOX_TYPE`; factory is `agent/utils/sandbox.py:create_sandbox` (`SANDBOX_FACTORIES` maps each provider name to a creator in `agent/integrations/`).\n\nEvery run re-applies `git config --global user.name/email` for the bot identity, because reused/reconnected sandboxes can lose `--global` config and Vercel preview deploys reject commits whose author email doesn't resolve to a GitHub account.\n\n### Middleware stack (order matters)\n\nConfigured in `agent/server.py:get_agent`, runs around every model call (in this order):\n\n1. `SanitizeToolInputsMiddleware` — strips/normalizes tool inputs before they reach tools.\n2. `ModelCallLimitMiddleware` (from `langchain.agents.middleware`) — caps model calls at `MODEL_CALL_RECURSION_LIMIT` (~half of `DEFAULT_RECURSION_LIMIT`); `exit_behavior=\"end\"`.\n3. `ToolErrorMiddleware` — catches tool exceptions and surfaces them as tool messages.\n4. `check_message_queue_before_model` — pulls Linear comments / Slack messages that arrived mid-run from the thread queue and injects them as user messages before the next LLM call. This is what makes \"message the agent while it's working\" work.\n5. `SlackAssistantStatusMiddleware` — keeps the Slack \"assistant is typing\"-style status up to date around model calls.\n6. `ensure_no_empty_msg` — after-model hook; when the model emits a message with no tool call (and hasn't already messaged the user or confirmed completion) it re-injects a synthetic `no_op` / `confirming_completion` tool call so the run continues instead of ending prematurely.\n7. `notify_step_limit_reached` — after-agent hook that posts a Slack reply when the agent hits the step limit, so the user gets a clear signal instead of silence.\n8. `SandboxCircuitBreakerMiddleware` — trips the agent out of repeated sandbox failures instead of looping.\n9. `ModelFallbackMiddleware` (optional, last) — added only when `LLM_FALLBACK_MODEL_ID` or the per-model default fallback differs from the primary model.\n\nThe system prompt instructs the agent to call a tool every turn, and `ensure_no_empty_msg` re-injects a tool call when it doesn't — together these keep runs from stopping partway through a task.\n\nOther middleware exists in `agent/middleware/` (`ExcludeToolsMiddleware`) but isn't wired into the default agent. The reviewer uses a leaner stack: `SanitizeToolInputsMiddleware`, `ModelCallLimitMiddleware`, `ToolErrorMiddleware`, `SlackAssistantStatusMiddleware`.\n\nThere is intentionally no after-agent safety net that opens a PR for the agent. The agent itself is responsible for committing, pushing, opening/updating the draft PR, and replying in the source channel — all via `GH_TOKEN=dummy gh` and `slack_thread_reply` / `linear_comment`.\n\n### Tools\n\nAll tools live in `agent/tools/` and are flat-imported via `agent/tools/__init__.py`. The set is intentionally small and curated — see README \"Tools — Curated, Not Accumulated\".\n\nWired into `get_agent`:\n`http_request`, `fetch_url`, `web_search`, `linear_comment`, `linear_create_issue`, `linear_delete_issue`, `linear_get_issue`, `linear_get_issue_comments`, `linear_list_teams`, `linear_update_issue`, `request_pr_review`, `schedule_thread_wakeup`, `slack_add_reaction`, `slack_read_thread_messages`, `slack_thread_reply`.\n\nReviewer-only tools (in `agent/reviewer.py`): `add_finding`, `update_finding`, `list_findings`, `publish_review`. The review-style analyzer uses `save_review_style` (exported as `save_review_style_prompt`).\n\nBuilt-in deepagents tools (`read_file`, `write_file`, `edit_file`, `ls`, `glob`, `grep`, `execute`, `write_todos`, `task` for subagent spawning, …) are added by `create_deep_agent` itself; don't duplicate them.\n\n### Models, profiles, and team defaults\n\nModel + reasoning effort are resolved per run in this precedence (highest wins):\n\n1. Per-thread config (`agent_model_id` + `agent_effort` in `configurable`) — set by webhooks/UI.\n2. Per-user dashboard profile override (`agent/dashboard/agent_overrides.py:load_profile`), keyed by resolved GitHub login.\n3. Team default model (`agent/dashboard/team_settings.py:get_team_default_model(\"agent\")`).\n\nSupported model IDs and per-model effort/reasoning rules live in `agent/dashboard/options.py`. Profile flags also drive run behavior — e.g. `profile_create_prs` enables the opt-in Always Create PRs policy. Model construction goes through `agent/utils/model.py` (`make_model`, `provider_model_kwargs`, `fallback_model_id_for`).\n\n### Auth\n\n- **GitHub**: dual-mode. User OAuth tokens are encrypted-at-rest in thread metadata (`agent/encryption.py`, `utils/auth.py:resolve_github_token`). When no user token is available, falls back to a GitHub App installation token (`utils/github_app.py`). The installation token is also what configures the LangSmith sandbox's GitHub proxy.\n- **Webhooks**: GitHub signatures verified in `utils/github_comments.py:verify_github_signature`; Slack/Linear handled in their respective utils.\n- **Dashboard / UI**: GitHub OAuth login lives in `agent/dashboard/oauth.py` and `routes.py` (`/auth/login`, `/auth/callback`, `/auth/logout`, `/me`).\n\n### Thread-id derivation\n\nWebhooks compute deterministic thread ids so the same Linear issue / Slack thread / PR routes back to the same running agent. See `utils/github_comments.py:get_thread_id_from_branch` and the equivalents in `utils/linear.py` / `utils/slack.py`. Reviewer threads have their own deterministic ids and are tagged with `REVIEWER_THREAD_KIND` metadata so the FastAPI side can find them.\n\n## Conventions\n\n- Tests are unit-only by default (`tests/`). Integration tests would go under `tests/integration_tests/` (currently empty — `make integration_tests` no-ops if missing).\n- New sandbox providers: add a module under `agent/integrations/` and wire it into `SANDBOX_FACTORIES` in `agent/utils/sandbox.py`. See `CUSTOMIZATION.md`.\n- New tools: add to `agent/tools/`, export from `agent/tools/__init__.py`, add to the `tools=[...]` list in `server.py:get_agent` (or `reviewer.py` for reviewer-only tools).\n- New middleware: add to `agent/middleware/`, export from `agent/middleware/__init__.py`, add to the `middleware=[...]` list in `server.py:get_agent` — order is significant (see the stack above).\n- New dashboard endpoints: add to `agent/dashboard/routes.py`. The router is auto-mounted on the FastAPI app.\n- New graphs: register the entrypoint in `langgraph.json` under `graphs`.\n- Minimal-to-no code comments — only when the *why* isn't obvious from the code.\n\nSummary: None\n\n\nTitle: 2529b10 fix: checkpoint per-run graph setup (#1690)\nURL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/commit/2529b1092b67a614f8b7bcbb37f5d82ce8994ac3\nID: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/commit/2529b1092b67a614f8b7bcbb37f5d82ce8994ac3\nScore: None\nPublished Date: None\nAuthor: None\nImage: None\nFavicon: None\nExtras: None\nSubpages: None\nCrawl Date: None\nText: # 2529b10 fix: checkpoint per-run graph setup (#1690)\n\n- SHA: 2529b1092b67a614f8b7bcbb37f5d82ce8994ac3\n- Repository: langchain-ai/open-swe\n- Author: johannes117\n- Date: 2026-07-06T18:51:43Z\n- +1158 -441 in 20 files\n- Verified: yes\n\n---\n\nfix: checkpoint per-run graph setup (#1690)\n\n* feat: prepare-run middleware infrastructure\n\n* refactor(agent): move per-run setup into PrepareAgentRunMiddleware\n\n* refactor(reviewer): move per-run setup into PrepareReviewerRunMiddleware\n\n* refactor(analyzer,chat): apply prepare pattern\n\n* perf: cache integration tool loading and model construction\n\n* test: prepare middleware and ttl cache coverage\n\n* test: mock reviewer model in token failure case\n\n* fix: scope prepare latch to run fingerprint\n\n---------\n\nCo-authored-by: open-swe[bot] <open-swe@users.noreply.github.com>\n\n## Changed Files\n\n| File | Status | + | - langchain-ai/open-swe at 3e7344792094561c4add68b22f59166880efdf39 · GitHub\nURL: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/tree/3e7344792094561c4add68b22f59166880efdf39\nID: https://github.com/langchain-ai/open-swe/tree/3e7344792094561c4add68b22f59166880efdf39\nScore: None\nPublished Date: 2025-05-21T21:44:24.000Z\nAuthor: langchain-ai\nImage: https://opengraph.githubassets.com/b6545a3389329f120b581a60e32268d91cb917c52e44672818a1cbadec313646/langchain-ai/open-swe\nFavicon: https://github.com/fluidicon.png\nExtras: None\nSubpages: None\nCrawl Date: None\nText: GitHub - langchain-ai/open-swe at 3e7344792094561c4add68b22f59166880efdf39 · GitHub\n\n/ open-swe Public\n\nNotifications\n\nStar 10.1k\n\n- Fork 1.2k\n\n3e73447\n\nBranches\n\nTags\n\nGo to Branches page\n\nGo to Tags page\n\nGo to file\n\nCode\n\nLast commit message\n\nLast commit date\n\n590 Commits\n\nView commit history for this file.\n\n.github/workflows\n\n.github/workflows\n\n.vscode\n\n.vscode\n\nagent\n\nagent\n\nstatic\n\nstatic\n\ntests\n\ntests\n\n.codespellignore\n\n.codespellignore\n\n.gitignore\n\n.gitignore\n\nCUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nCUSTOMIZATION.md\n\nDockerfile\n\nDockerfile\n\nINSTALLATION.md\n\nINSTALLATION.md\n\nLICENSE\n\nLICENSE\n\nMakefile\n\nMakefile\n\nREADME.md\n\nREADME.md\n\nSECURITY.md\n\nSECURITY.md\n\nlanggraph.json\n\nlanggraph.json\n\npyproject.toml\n\npyproject.toml\n\nuv.lock\n\nuv.lock\n\nView all files\n\n| Name | Name |\n| --- | --- |\n\n## Repository files navigation\n\n# Open SWE\n\nThe open-source framework for building your org's internal coding agent\n\nElite engineering orgs like Stripe, Ramp, and Coinbase are building their own internal coding agents — Slackbots, CLIs, and web apps that meet engineers where they already work. These agents are connected to internal systems with the right context, permissioning, and safety boundaries to operate with minimal human oversight.\n\nOpen SWE is the open-source version of this pattern. Built on LangGraph and Deep Agents, it gives you the same architecture those companies built internally: cloud sandboxes, Slack and Linear invocation, subagent orchestration, and automatic PR creation — ready to customize for your own codebase and workflows.\n\nNote\n\n💬 Read the announcement blog post here\n\n---\n\n## Architecture\n\nOpen SWE makes the same core architectural decisions as the best internal coding agents. Here's how it maps to the patterns described in this overview of Stripe's Minions, Ramp's Inspect, and Coinbase's Cloudbot:\n\n### 1. Agent Harness — Composed on Deep Agents\n\nRather than forking an existing agent or building from scratch, Open SWE composes on the Deep Agents framework — similar to how Ramp built on top of OpenCode. This gives you an upgrade path (pull in upstream improvements) while letting you customize the orchestration, tools, and middleware for your org.\n\n```\ncreate_deep_agent(\n    model=\"anthropic:claude-opus-4-6\",\n    system_prompt=construct_system_prompt(repo_dir, ...),\n    tools=[http_request, fetch_url, commit_and_open_pr, linear_comment, slack_thread_reply],\n    backend=sandbox_backend,\n    middleware=[ToolErrorMiddleware(), check_message_queue_before_model, ...],\n)\n```\n\n### 2. Sandbox — Isolated Cloud Environments\n\nEvery task runs in its own isolated cloud sandbox — a remote Linux environment with full shell access. The repo is cloned in, the agent gets full permissions, and the blast radius of any mistake is fully contained. No production access, no confirmation prompts.\n\nOpen SWE supports multiple sandbox providers out of the box — Modal, Daytona, Runloop, and LangSmith— and you can plug in your own. See the Customization Guide for details.\n\nThis follows the principle all three companies converge on: isolate first, then give full permissions inside the boundary.\n\n- Each thread gets a persistent sandbox (reused across follow-up messages)\n- Sandboxes auto-recreate if they become unreachable\n- Multiple tasks run in parallel — each in its own sandbox, no queuing\n\n### 3. Tools — Curated, Not Accumulated\n\nStripe's key insight: tool curation matters more than tool quantity. Open SWE follows this principle with a small, focused toolset:\n\n| Tool | Purpose |\n| --- | --- |\n| `execute` | Shell commands in the sandbox |\n| `fetch_url` | Fetch web pages as markdown |\n| `http_request` | API calls (GET, POST, etc.) |\n| `commit_and_open_pr` | Git commit + open a GitHub draft PR |\n| `linear_comment` | Post updates to Linear tickets |\n| `slack_thread_reply` | Reply in Slack threads |\n\nPlus the built-in Deep Agents tools:`read_file`,`write_file`,`edit_file`,`ls`,`glob`,`grep`,`write_todos`, and`task`(subagent spawning).\n\n### 4. Context Engineering — AGENTS.md + Source Context\n\nOpen SWE gathers context from two sources:\n\n- `AGENTS.md`— If the repo contains an`AGENTS.md` file at the root, it's read from the sandbox and injected into the system prompt. This is your repo-level equivalent of Stripe's rule files: encoding conventions, testing requirements, and architectural decisions that every agent run should follow.\n- Source context — The full Linear issue (title, description, comments) or Slack thread history is assembled and passed to the agent, so it starts with rich context rather than discovering everything through tool calls.\n\n### 5. Orchestration — Subagents + Middleware\n\nOpen SWE's orchestration has two layers:\n\nSubagents: The Deep Agents framework natively supports spawning child agents via the`task` tool. The main agent can fan out independent subtasks to isolated subagents — each with its own middleware stack, todo list, and file operations. This is similar to Ramp's child sessions for parallel work.\n\nMiddleware: Deterministic middleware hooks run around the agent loop:\n\n- `check_message_queue_before_model`— Injects follow-up messages (Linear comments or Slack messages that arrive mid-run) before the next model call. You can message the agent while it's working and it'll pick up your input at its next step.\n- `open_pr_if_needed`— After-agent safety net that commits and opens a PR if the agent didn't do it itself. This is a lightweight version of Stripe's deterministic nodes — ensuring critical steps happen regardless of LLM behavior.\n- `ToolErrorMiddleware`— Catches and handles tool errors gracefully.\n\n### 6. Invocation — Slack, Linear, and GitHub\n\nAll three companies in the article converge on Slack as the primary invocation surface. Open SWE does the same:\n\n- Slack — Mention the bot in any thread. Supports`repo:owner/name` syntax to specify which repo to work on. The agent replies in-thread with status updates and PR links.\n- Linear — Comment`@openswe` on any issue. The agent reads the full issue context, reacts with 👀 to acknowledge, and posts results back as comments.\n- GitHub — Tag`@openswe` in PR comments on agent-created PRs to have it address review feedback and push fixes to the same branch.\n\nEach invocation creates a deterministic thread ID, so follow-up messages on the same issue or thread route to the same running agent.\n\n### 7. Validation — Prompt-Driven + Safety Nets\n\nThe agent is instructed to run linters, formatters, and tests before committing. The`open_pr_if_needed` middleware acts as a backstop — if the agent finishes without opening a PR, the middleware handles it automatically.\n\nThis is an area where you can extend Open SWE for your org: add deterministic CI checks, visual verification, or review gates as additional middleware. See the Customization Guide for how.\n\n---\n\n## Comparison\n\n| Decision | Open SWE | Stripe (Minions) | Ramp (Inspect) | Coinbase (Cloudbot) |\n| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |\n| Harness | Composed (Deep Agents/LangGraph) | Forked (Goose) | Composed (OpenCode) | Built from scratch |\n| Sandbox | Pluggable (Modal, Daytona, Runloop, etc.) | AWS EC2 devboxes (pre-warmed) | Modal containers (pre-warmed) | In-house |\n| Tools | ~15, curated | ~500, curated per-agent | OpenCode SDK + extensions | MCPs + custom Skills |\n| Context | AGENTS.md + issue/thread | Rule files + pre-hydration | OpenCode built-in | Linear-first + MCPs |\n| Orchestration | Subagents + middleware | Blueprints (deterministic + agentic) | Sessions + child sessions | Three modes |\n| Invocation | Slack, Linear, GitHub | Slack + embedded buttons | Slack + web + Chrome extension | Slack-native |\n| Validation | Prompt-driven + PR safety net | 3-layer (local + CI + 1 retry) | Visual DOM verification | Agent councils + auto-merge |\n\n---\n\n## Features\n\n- Trigger from Linear, Slack, or GitHub — mention`@openswe` in a comment to kick off a task\n- Instant acknowledgement — reacts with 👀 the moment it picks up your message\n- Message it while it's running — send follow-up messages mid-task and it'll pick them up before its next step\n- Run multiple tasks in parallel — each task runs in its own isolated cloud sandbox\n- GitHub OAuth built-in — authenticates with your GitHub account automatically\n- Opens PRs automatically — commits changes and opens a draft PR when done, linked back to your ticket\n- Subagent support — the agent can spawn child agents for parallel subtasks\n\n---\n\n## Getting Started\n\n- Installation Guide— GitHub App creation, LangSmith, Linear/Slack/GitHub triggers, and production deployment\n- Customization Guide— swap the sandbox, model, tools, triggers, system prompt, and middleware for your org\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n\n## About\n\nAn Open-Source Asynchronous
